2: veatta Beit-Lechem Efrata tzair lihyot be'alfei Yehudah mimcha li yetze lihyot moshel beYisrael umotza'otav mikedem miyemei olam: 'But you, O Bethlehem Ephrathah, who are too little to be among the clans of Judah, from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Israel, whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days.' Motza'otav mikedem miyemei olam (whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days/from ancient times/from everlasting): the preposition and nouns can point to either the antiquity of the Davidic dynasty (David's line goes back to ancient Bethlehem) or to the eternal pre-existence of the coming ruler. The NT reading (John 8:58; John 1:1) takes it in the latter, stronger sense: the one born in Bethlehem has his 'goings forth' from eternity.
1: Now muster your troops, O daughter of troops — atah titgodedi bat-gedud: wordplay — gedud (troops/raiding party) echoes mitgodedi (muster yourself). The siege is underway: matsor sam 'aleinu (a siege has been laid against us). Ba-shevet yakkbu 'al-ha-lechi et shofet Yisra'el (with a rod they strike the ruler of Israel on the cheek) — striking the cheek was the ultimate public humiliation (1 Kings 22:24; Job 16:10; Lamentations 3:30); the ruler's humiliation by the enemy sets up the contrast with the coming ruler from Bethlehem (v2).
3: Therefore he will give them up until the time when she who is in labor gives birth — lachen yitnem 'ad-'et yoledah yaladah: the labor-birth motif from Micah 4:9-10 continues; the birth-pangs of exile precede the delivery of restoration. Ve-yeter echav yashuvu (the rest of his brothers shall return) — the restoration of the scattered northern tribes alongside the southern remnant; the re-unification of all Israel under the Bethlehem ruler.
4: He shall stand and shepherd his flock in the strength of the LORD — ve-'amad u-ra'ah be-'oz YHWH: three elements of the coming ruler's activity: 'amad (stand, be established), ra'ah (shepherd/feed), and the strength-source — be-'oz YHWH (in YHWH's strength) and bi-ge'ut shem YHWH Elohav (in the majesty of the name of the LORD his God). Ve-yashavu ki-atah yigdal ad-afssei arets (and they shall dwell secure, for now he shall be great to the ends of the earth) — global greatness of the shepherd-ruler.
5: And this one shall be their peace — ve-hayah zeh shalom: a remarkable statement — the person himself (zeh = this one) is shalom (peace, wholeness). Peace is not an attribute of his rule but his identity. The Assyrian invasion that follows in vv5b-6 is countered not by superior military force but by the one who embodies shalom. Shiva'h ro'im u-shmonah nesikhei adam (seven shepherds and eight human commanders) — the graduated numerical formula (x, x+1) from Amos 1-2 indicating sufficiency; seven-plus-eight represents complete adequacy of response.
6: They shall rule the land of Assyria with the sword, and the land of Nimrod — Nimrod (the land of Nimrod) is Mesopotamia/Babylon (Genesis 10:8-12); by v5's time Babylon had absorbed the Assyrian empire. The prophecy operates on two levels: immediate (the Assyrian crisis of 701 BC) and eschatological (the ultimate defeat of all anti-covenant powers represented by Assyria/Nimrod).
7: The remnant of Jacob shall be in the midst of many peoples, like dew from the LORD — ke-tal me'et YHWH: dew (tal) in the ANE was a gift of divine provision — it came without human effort, unpredictably, sustaining vegetation when rain was absent. Ka-revivim 'alei esev (like showers on the grass) — the remnant's presence among nations is beneficial and life-giving, not aggressive. Asher lo-yekavveh le-ish ve-lo yayachel li-vene adam (which do not wait for a man or tarry for the children of man) — the blessing comes from divine initiative alone, not human arrangement.
8: The remnant of Jacob shall be among the nations, like a lion — the two remnant-images (vv7-8) are strikingly contrasted: the same remnant is both dew (gentle, life-giving) and lion (powerful, destroying). Ka-arye be-vehemot ya'ar (like a lion among the beasts of the forest) and ke-khefir be-'adrei tson (like a young lion among flocks of sheep) — the lion tears and there is no rescuer. The duality of the remnant's role reflects the dual nature of the Messiah's coming: gentle shepherd and consuming judge.
9: Your hand shall be lifted up over your adversaries — terum yadkha 'al-tsarrekha: the uplifted hand of victory; the standard gesture of military triumph (Exodus 17:11). Ve-khol-oyvekha yikkarettu (and all your enemies shall be cut off) — comprehensive enemy-elimination.
10: And in that day, declares the LORD, I will cut off your horses from among you — ve-hikhrati susekha: the divine purification of Israel begins with military hardware. Susim (horses) and merkavot (chariots) were prohibited to the Israelite king by Deuteronomy 17:16 ('he shall not acquire many horses for himself'); relying on cavalry was a form of covenant unfaithfulness. The cutting off of military power is not punishment here but liberation from false security.
11: I will cut off the cities of your land and tear down all your strongholds — ve-hikhrati 'arei artsekha: the fortified cities and strongholds (mibtsar) represent the defensive infrastructure built instead of trusting YHWH. Each thing cut off in vv10-14 corresponds to something that replaced genuine covenant trust.
12: I will cut off sorcery from your hand — ve-hikhrati khesafim: keshafim (sorcery, magical practices) — divination and magic as alternative means of knowledge/control that competed with prophetic revelation. Ve-'ovenat lo-yihyu lakh (you shall have no more fortune-tellers) — 'onen (fortune-teller, cloud-reader, omen-interpreter) — the complete elimination of the divinatory infrastructure.
13: I will cut off your carved images and your stone pillars — pesilim (carved images) and matstsevot (sacred standing stones/pillars) — the matstsevah (standing stone) was originally acceptable (Genesis 28:18; Micah 31:45) but became associated with Canaanite fertility worship and prohibited (Deuteronomy 16:22). Ve-lo-tishtachaveh od le-ma'aseh yadekha (you shall no longer bow down to the work of your own hands) — the criterion: handmade objects that receive worship.
14: I will root up your Asherah poles from among you and destroy your cities — asherayikh (Asherah poles) — wooden poles or stylized trees representing the Canaanite goddess Asherah, consort of El; planted near altars (Deuteronomy 16:21; 1 Kings 14:23; Micah 16:33). The consistent coupling of Baal and Asherah worship throughout Kings confirms their paired popularity. Their rooting up (nateshthi) is thorough agricultural clearance.
15: And in anger and wrath I will execute vengeance upon the nations that did not obey — ve-'asiti be-af u-ve-chamat naqam et-ha-goyim: the divine purification that began with Israel's own military/cultic structures (vv10-14) turns outward to the nations. The double emotion — af (anger/nose) and chamath (heat, wrath) — intensifies the final judgment. The nations are held accountable to the same moral standard as Israel.
